sections.heading: Overview
sections.paragraph: The pharmaceutical landscape for targeted protein degradation is evolving rapidly beyond traditional proteolysis targeting chimeras (PROTACs) to include molecular glues and next-generation proximity medicines. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration greenlit Arvinas and Pfizer’s vepdegestrant, the first PROTAC approved for advanced breast cancer, marking a regulatory milestone for the modality. Concurrently, significant capital is flowing into alternative degradation technologies, with Merck committing $2.55 billion to Proxygen for molecular glue degraders targeting multiple therapeutic areas. AbbVie, Eli Lilly, and Biogen have also secured substantial partnerships with Neomorph to develop glues for immunology and cancer. While PROTACs utilize chemical linkers to bind target proteins and E3 ligases, molecular glues offer smaller molecular weights and blood-brain barrier penetration, as seen in Proxygen’s CDK12 program. Halda Therapeutics, owned by Johnson & Johnson, is advancing RIPTACs that employ a “hold and kill” mechanism distinct from proteasome-dependent degradation. Fortitude Biomedicines is exploring Glue DACs to overcome antibody-drug conjugate resistance. These developments underscore a strategic shift toward drugging undruggable targets through induced proximity, with companies like General Proximity utilizing platforms to scan effector proteins for oncology and neurodegeneration applications.

points.text: Arvinas — Vepdegestrant — Advanced Breast Cancer — FDA greenlit first-ever PROTAC for treatment.
Merck — Proxygen — Multiple Targets — $2.55 billion deal to identify and develop molecular glue degraders.
Halda Therapeutics — HLD-0915 — Cancer — RIPTAC entered clinic with encouraging early results.
Arvinas: Developing PROTACs including vepdegestrant for advanced breast cancer.
Proxygen: Developing next-generation molecular glue degraders targeting p300 and CDK12.
Halda Therapeutics: Pioneering RIPTACs with a hold and kill mechanism for cancer.
Fortitude Biomedicines: Developing Glue DACs to overcome ADC drug resistance.
Vepdegestrant: PROTAC mechanism, oestrogen receptor degrader, advanced breast cancer, FDA approved.
Proxygen p300: Covalent pharmacology, suppresses oncogenic transcriptional programs, IND-enabling studies next year.
Proxygen CDK12: Eliminates cyclin K, high brain penetrance, targets brain metastases, IND-enabling studies next year.
HLD-0915: RIPTAC mechanism, binds target and pan-essential protein, oral small molecule, entered clinic last year.
Merck and Proxygen: $2.55 billion deal to identify and develop molecular glue degraders against multiple therapeutic targets.
AbbVie and Neomorph: $1.64 billion deal to create glues for immunology and cancer targets.
Eli Lilly and Neomorph: $1.45 billion pact to develop molecular glues.
Biogen and Neomorph: $1.46 billion pact to develop molecular glues.
Novartis and Monte Rosa Therapeutics: $5.7 billion deal to license an undisclosed discovery target.
Takeda and Degron Therapeutics: $1.2 billion licensing deal to advance degraders for oncology, neuroscience, and inflammation.
Fortitude Biomedicines: Launched with $13 million in seed funding in 2026.
